roots.title: Introduction to Modeling, Loss Functions, and Data Polling
roots.summary: The lecture introduces the modeling phase of Data 100, focusing on mathematical derivations, model building, and performance evaluation, starting with simple linear regression and advancing to multiple regression. Models are defined as idealized representations summarizing trends, with an emphasis on the importance of modeling assumptions, especially in populations with incomplete data. A quote by George Box highlights that while all models are wrong, some are useful. The discussion includes modeling complex relationships, such as the correlation between parents' heights and other variables, advocating for simple, interpretable models, and mentions the use of deep learning for high accuracy with large datasets, along with challenges in interpretability. The significance of accurately classifying emails for spam detection is also covered. An active poll engages participants in selecting the line that best matches given data from three options. Additionally, the segment discusses loss functions in data modeling, emphasizing their role in measuring prediction accuracy by quantifying the error between predicted and actual values, and the importance of choosing appropriate loss functions for different tasks, including L2 loss for regression.

children.title: Introduction to Modeling and Complex Relationships
children.summary: The lecture introduces the modeling phase of Data 100, focusing on mathematical derivations, model building, and performance evaluation, starting with a review of simple linear regression and advancing to multiple regression. Models are defined as idealized representations that summarize trends, acknowledging that they may not capture all details or noise. The importance of modeling assumptions is discussed, particularly in the context of populations with incomplete data, emphasizing that not all data points will align with the model. A quote by George Box is highlighted, stating that while all models are wrong, some are useful. The lecture also covers modeling complex relationships, such as the correlation between parents' heights and other variables, advocating for simple, interpretable models. The use of deep learning for high accuracy with large datasets is mentioned, along with the challenges of interpretability. The significance of accurately classifying emails, particularly in spam detection, is discussed, outlining reasons for developing models to explain phenomena, predict outcomes, and make causal inferences.

children.title: Introduction to Modeling in Data 100
children.summary: The lecture begins with a welcome and mentions a technical issue with Zoom. It will focus on the modeling phase of Data 100, covering mathematical derivations, model building, and performance evaluation. The session will start with a review of simple linear regression from Data 8, advancing to more complex models, including a discussion on multiple regression. A model is defined as an idealized representation of a system, which may not capture all details or noise but aims to summarize trends accurately.

children.title: Introduction to Modeling in Data 100
children.summary: The lecture begins with a welcome and a note about a technical issue with Zoom. The focus of the upcoming lectures will be on the modeling phase of Data 100, including mathematical derivations, model building, and performance evaluation. The session will start with a review of simple linear regression from Data 8, progressing to more advanced models, culminating in a discussion on multiple regression. A model is defined as an idealized representation of a system, acknowledging that it may not capture all details or noise but aims to summarize trends accurately.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: All right, welcome data 100. This is going to be a very fun lecture. So just before I start, it looks like that our Zoom has an issue that it stops itself before the started lecture. So we're trying to get that fixed. So thank you for all of those who are online and they rejoined the Zoom webinar again. So kind of telling you, we're at today and we're going in the next couple of lectures is that we're starting actually digging deeper into the modeling phase of data 100. So it's going to be really exciting. I'm going to try to do a lot of math derivation and also building different models with you and also evaluating different, the performance of different models with you throughout today's lecture and also in the next two lectures to come. So this is where we are. We're going to start by reviewing what you've seen in in data 8, we're going to start by taking a look at what was the simple linear regression and what is modeling and what not. And we're going to go a little bit deeper into more advanced models up to the point that in the last lecture of the regression sequence, we're going to talk about multiple regression. So what happens when you have multiple different attributes that you want to model and you want to combine them into predicting one variable? So this is a roadmap for today. We're going to start by talking about what is a model. Most of you might know what is a model or like you've used the term that there is a machine learning model and whatnot before, but just to tell you what a model is from our perspective in data 100 is that we always think about a model as an idealized representation of a system. So we say that it's an idealized representation of the system because it's not always accurate, we're not always considering all the details and all different features and all the noise that's involved in the system, but we're trying to actually have a model that kind of simulates or summarizes the trend that's happening in a system as accurately as possible. So an example of that could be modeling the fall of an object, an Earth subject to constant gravity that we always think about that as the gravity as being 9.81 meter per second square, but we all know that depending on the elevation, depending on the air resistance, this number is not accurate. A model is an idealized representation of a system.
1380664
Example:
We model the fall of an object on Earth as subject to a constant
acceleration of 9.81 m/s2 due to gravity.
While this describes the behavior of our system, it is merely
an approximation.
It doesn't account for the effects of air resistance, local
variations in gravity, etc.
But in practice, it's accurate enough to be useful!

A model is an idealized representation of a system.
1380664
Example:
We model the fall of an object on Earth as subject to a constant
acceleration of 9.81 m/s2 due to gravity.
While this describes the behavior of our system, it is merely
an approximation.
It doesn't account for the effects of air resistance, local
variations in gravity, etc.
But in practice, it's accurate enough to be useful!

children.title: Modeling Assumptions and Complex Phenomena
children.summary: Models are idealized representations that simplify system dynamics for usability. In data 100, the focus will be on modeling populations with incomplete data, assuming samples adequately represent the population. Not all data points will align with the model, indicating noise that is often not directly modeled. A quote by George Box emphasizes that while all models are wrong, some are useful, highlighting the importance of models in understanding complex phenomena.

children.title: Modeling Assumptions and Complex Phenomena
children.summary: Models are idealized representations of systems that simplify underlying dynamics for usability. In data 100, the focus will be on modeling populations despite incomplete data, assuming that samples represent the population well. Not all data points will fit the model perfectly, representing noise that is often not modeled directly. A quote by George Box highlights that while all models are wrong, some are useful, emphasizing the necessity of building models to understand complex phenomena.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So it's not going to be a constant model or a constant valley that we can use in order to model the gravity. So this is an example that we simplify assumptions, so that we have a model that's kind of roughly accurate. It's usable. It's simplifying all the dynamics of the, the dynamics in the underlying nature of the system that we have. And that's actually what we're going to do in data 100 as well. Because what you've seen in the previous lecture is that we want to model for population, but we don't have the population. We're trying to simplify assumptions, say that okay, we're sampling. Our dataset is a good sample and is a good representation of the population. And now we're going to do the modeling. And not all of our data points are going to follow the trend that we're going to to model. So those are kind of the noises that we're trying to also avoid or not directly model. So there's a famous saying by George Box that says essentially all mothers are wrung but some are useful, which is exactly what you should expect from all the modeling work that we'll be doing in data 100 as well. we want to build models. children.title: Modeling Complex Relationships in Data
children.summary: The discussion covers modeling complex relationships, such as the correlation between parents' average heights and other variables, emphasizing the importance of simple, interpretable models that still achieve reasonable accuracy. It highlights the use of deep learning architectures for high accuracy with large datasets, despite their 'black box' nature, where the relationships between parameters are not easily identifiable. An example of spam email detection is mentioned, linking it to a project in the Data 100 course.

children.title: Modeling Complex Relationships in Data
children.summary: The discussion focuses on modeling complex relationships, such as the correlation between parents' average heights and other variables. It emphasizes the need for simple yet interpretable models that maintain reasonable accuracy. To achieve high accuracy, especially with large datasets, deep learning architectures may be employed, which involve numerous parameters, making them 'black box' models. Although the relationships between parameters are not easily identifiable, these models can produce more accurate predictions than simpler, interpretable models. An example provided is spam email detection, which is related to a project in the Data 100 course.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So for example, we want to figure out our parents average heights related to chillers average heights. So there's like all of those complex relationships that we can study directly, but we can actually collect a bunch of data and then model based on or create a model based on those assumptions or those data points that we have. So in this case, what we would say is that the concept that we're modeling is very complex. What we want to have is a very simple model that is also interpretable, but it's also at the same time reasonably accurate. We don't want to try and random kind of estimations of the values that we want. The second reason is that We might want to have really accurate productions we have a good amount of data we want to make sure that we have very good accurate productions and in doing so what we will do is we'll throw in multiple different parameters not multiple but millions of parameters into a deep learning architecture for example in order to have a very accurate production. So what's happening when we want to use these black box models, we call them black box models because there's millions of different parameters. It's not easily, we cannot easily identify what's the relationship between them or what is the interaction between different parameters that is bits out, the actual estimation that we have at the end. But what we know is that over the population or over the sample that we have, we would say that it's accurate enough that we can actually use it. And it's more accurate than most of the interpretable models or simple models that we've seen in the first case. So examples could be if you want to have a spam email detection. So this is kind of a project that you will do in the last project of data 100. Video: 1: X

children.title: Email Classification and Model Accuracy
children.summary: The segment discusses the significance of accurately classifying emails as spam or not, stressing the need to prevent the misclassification of important messages. It outlines three key reasons for developing models: to explain complex phenomena, to predict outcomes for unseen data, and to make causal inferences. Additionally, it highlights the trade-off between model interpretability and prediction accuracy, especially regarding the use of black-box models in deep learning.

children.title: Importance of Accurate Email Classification
children.summary: The segment discusses the task of classifying emails as spam or not spam, emphasizing the importance of accuracy to avoid misclassifying important emails. It introduces three reasons for building models: to explain complex phenomena, to make accurate predictions about unseen data, and to make causal inferences. The trade-off between model interpretability and prediction accuracy is highlighted, particularly in the context of black-box models commonly used in deep learning.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We're going to give you emails with subject line, with the text in the body, and then you will be classifying emails into class of a spam versus not a spam. What you want is you really want to be accurate here. You don't want to have a spam email landing in your inbox or vice versa, missing an important email and moving that into your spam folder. So this is very important. In these cases, we would say that the reason that we want to do the model is to have a very accurate prediction, even if it comes at the cost of the model, not being interpretable. children.title: Causal Inference and Model Interpretability
children.summary: Causal inference is crucial for understanding variable relationships, such as determining causation versus correlation, exemplified by smoking and lung cancer. In Data 100, the focus is on modeling functions that connect inputs to outputs, emphasizing the balance between model interpretability and accuracy. While some models may prioritize accuracy over interpretability, the goal is to achieve both. Common model types discussed include mechanistic or deterministic models, which rely on non-probabilistic measurements and historical scientific laws.

children.title: Causal Inference and Model Interpretability
children.summary: Causal inference is a concept not covered in Data 100, but it is essential for understanding relationships between variables, such as determining if smoking causes lung cancer rather than just correlating the two. The focus in Data 100 is on modeling functions that map inputs to outputs. A key point is the balance between interpretability and accuracy in models; while some models may lack interpretability, the ideal scenario is to have a model that is both interpretable and accurate. Common model types include mechanistic or deterministic models, which are based on non-probabilistic measurements and relationships, exemplified by historical scientific laws like Kepler's and Newton's.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Reason tree is a reason that we will not go over in data 100, but that's a topic for more advanced courses, some stats courses or data 102 covers causal inference. So for example, if you want to figure out if a smoking causes cancers, not correlation between a smoking and a person having lung cancer, but figuring out the causation between causation relationship between a variable and another variable or a response output, in those cases, we do causal inference. Again, not the topic for data 100, but just so that you know that everything that we're modeling in a stage one and two, it's mostly around finding a function that maps input to an output. But what we're looking at in the recent tree is to actually have a causal inference kind of argument and figuring out if a variable not only explains the output, but also causes the output. Again, others go pernata 100. Throughout the explanation of this reason one, two, three, I tried to say that interpretability is important and also accuracy is important. So what we'll try to do generally as data scientists is to strike the balance between interpretability and accuracy. So you really don't want to have a model, I mean, in some cases, we do have models that are not interpretable, but the best case scenario would be that If you have an intrapartibled model that is at the same time is accurate enough for you to use as well. So, common types of models. There are mechanistic models or deterministic models. These are models that generally follow a non-probabilistic view, meaning that there is a study or there are measurements, mechanistic measurements that we would do, such as, for example, the way that people arrived at Kepler's, for the law of planetary motion or Newton's law. And based on those measurements, they built a relationship between multiple different variables and they built those models. We call these models mechanistic or deterministic models. We call them deterministic because there is no probability involved. When they measure, they measure it, the values is there, and then they measure multiple different variables and they basically look at the relationship between them. So what we'll try to do generally as data scientists is to strike the balance between interpretability and accuracy. So you really don't want to have a model, I mean, in some cases, we do have models that are not interpretable, but the best case scenario would be that If you have an intrapartibled model that is at the same time is accurate enough for you to use as well. So, common types of models. There are mechanistic models or deterministic models. These are models that generally follow a non-probabilistic view, meaning that there is a study or there are measurements, mechanistic measurements that we would do, such as, for example, the way that people arrived at Kepler's, for the law of planetary motion or Newton's law. And based on those measurements, they built a relationship between multiple different variables and they built those models. We call these models mechanistic or deterministic models. We call them deterministic because there is no probability involved. When they measure, they measure it, the values is there, and then they measure multiple different variables and they basically look at the relationship between them.

children.title: Probabilistic Models in Brain Wave Analysis
children.summary: Probabilistic models are crucial for classification tasks in brain wave analysis, particularly for assessing anxiety levels. An example highlights the use of these models to predict seizure onset by treating brain waves as random processes, which helps determine the likelihood of a patient experiencing a seizure soon. This allows for enhanced monitoring in medical settings. The discussion also contrasts probabilistic models with mechanistic or deterministic models, which lack probability incorporation.

children.title: Probabilistic Models in Brain Wave Analysis
children.summary: Probabilistic models are essential for classification tasks, particularly in studying brain waves to assess anxiety levels. An example from research involves modeling brain waves as random processes to predict the likelihood of seizure onset based on recorded data over time. These models can indicate the probability of a patient experiencing a seizure in the near future, allowing for closer monitoring in medical facilities. The discussion contrasts probabilistic models with mechanistic or deterministic models, which do not incorporate probability.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: There are models that are probabilistic. You will see one example of a probabilistic model towards the end of data 100, and mostly we will see probabilistic models when we want to do classification or identification of a certain task. So an example of that, I brought it here from one of my papers that is the work of my PhD student looking into studying brain waves in order to figure out the anxiety levels of subjects. So brain waves by themselves are random processes that you're modeling. There are probabilistic models that basically you will try to study and figure out how they travel in brain and how they interact with each other and how would they concentrate in a certain area so that it causes anxiety or it causes, for example, seizure. This is kind of the modeling that they would do in order to predict whether someone is going to have an onset of seizure in future or not just looking at those probabilistic models. And then you will basically have a probabilistic model that will say that with this specific likelihood, this subject that we had to recording up their brain waves over the last, like for example, seven days is going to have seizure in, for example, in the next 10 minutes. And if that probability is high enough then basically in patient facilities, they'll try to monitor that patient more accurately or more closely. So this is the kind of different categories of models that we're looking at. Mechanistic or deterministic, there's no probability involved and probabilistic models that again you will see later on in data 100. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Introduction to Simple Linear Regression and Residuals
children.summary: This segment introduces simple linear regression, referencing a previous example involving the prediction of character appearances in 'Little Women' based on punctuation counts. It outlines the regression line's equation, defining the slope as the correlation coefficient (R) multiplied by the ratio of standard deviations of Y and X, and the intercept as the average of Y minus the product of the slope and the average of X. The importance of understanding these concepts rather than memorizing equations is emphasized, along with the use of calculus for deriving the regression line. The segment also explains regression estimation, where values are predicted using the model, and introduces residuals as the difference between observed and estimated values, indicating prediction accuracy. It clarifies that positive residuals occur for points above the regression line and negative residuals for points below, with the goal of minimizing these residuals to fit the model closely to the data.

children.title: Introduction to Simple Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment introduces simple linear regression, referencing a previous example from data 8 involving the prediction of character appearances in chapters of 'Little Women' based on punctuation counts. It outlines the regression line's equation and the concepts of slope and intercept. The slope is defined as the correlation coefficient (R) multiplied by the ratio of the standard deviations of Y and X, while the intercept is calculated as the average of Y minus the product of the slope and the average of X. The segment emphasizes the importance of understanding these concepts rather than memorizing equations, and mentions the use of calculus for deriving the regression line.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What we're going to do in data 100 right now as a starting point, just to get warmed up as to take a look at simple linear regression and what you'd see in data 8. So we're going to try go over regression line and the definition for regression line and actually derive the equation for regression line that you'd seen. But this time we're going to actually use a little bit of calculus in this lecture to just get warm up because you will need to get comfortable with actually doing derivatives and calculating or optimizing an objective function and whatnot. I'm going to tell you what objective function is by the end of today's lecture. So let's dive in. You've seen this regression line from before, right? So you've seen that in data aid, this was a famous plot that you had that was predicting on different chapters of little women novel that using a number of periods, period punctuations in a chapter, it was trying to predict the number of characters that would appear in that specific chapter. You would see that it tries to modulate using regression line and then we're going to take a look at the equations for that regression line and HUD data aid arrived at that regression line. You also see that in data aid, we defined a slope as correlation or R times the standard deviation of Y over the standard deviation of X. This is your Y dimension and this is your X dimension and correlation. I'm going to formula define it in a sec. An intercept, we talked about intercept or the point that when X is equal to zero, your Y value is non-zero probably, or that's called Y intercept or intercept was defined as average of Y minus slope times average of X. And we're going to see why that's the case. So you don't need to actually memorize all of these equations anymore. You just need to know two things about a model in order to drive all the equations yourself. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Regression Estimation and Residuals Explained
children.summary: Regression estimation involves using a model to predict values by plugging inputs into the equation, which consists of an intercept plus the slope times the input value. The concept of residuals, defined as the observed value minus the estimated value, is crucial in this process. Residuals, which can be positive or negative, indicate the accuracy of predictions; points not on the regression line represent imperfect predictions. The goal in data modeling is to minimize these residuals, ensuring the model fits the data as closely as possible. A formal definition of residuals will be provided later.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So you're going to be released from memorizing kind of task from this point in time. We also talked about regression estimation. So regression estimation is that, okay, you have a model or estimation on any model, per se, is that we have a model and we're gonna just plug in the value that we wanna do prediction on into the equation and the equation was intercept plus slope times the value and then that would give you the regression estimation. There is a concept of residual that we're going to play a lot with and residual is the fine as observed value minus the estimated value. Okay, so the residuals are actually shown here on the plot. This is a residual. This is a residual. This is a residual and so on and so forth. What we're trying to do in data 100 and true out the modeling is that we're looking at the residuals that we're accumulating true out the entire data set and we're trying to minimize the amount of residual and I'm going to define what amount of residual means formally using a function mathematical function later on in today's lecture, but just know that if a point is not on the line, that's an imperfect prediction and we're going to measure how imperfect it is and we're going to try to make sure that the model that we're using is going to be a real we're fitting to the data is passing through all the data points as accurately as possible. children.title: Understanding Residuals and Regression Line
children.summary: The segment explains the concept of residuals in regression analysis, highlighting that a positive residual occurs for points above the regression line and a negative residual for points below it. It emphasizes the relationship between positive and negative residuals, suggesting that the regression line typically passes through the center of the data points. Additionally, it defines the regression line as the unique straight line that minimizes the mean squared error of estimation, with the formula for the regression estimate given as intercept plus slope times x.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is my ground truth why and this is why hat. I put hat as a representation of this is the predicted value or the estimated value. And you can see that based on this equation, the residual is a positive value for the points that are above the line. And you can also rationalize in the same way that for any point that's below the line, the residual is a negative value. You will see later on that there is a relationship between all of these residuals that are positive under residuals that are negative. And that's why the line generally passes through in the middle of the data point. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: Understanding Correlation and Regression Line
children.summary: Correlation is defined as the average of the product of X and Y when both are measured in standard units, which means they are zero-centered with a standard deviation of one. To standardize data, calculate the average and subtract it from each individual sample. The regression line is described as the unique straight line that minimizes the mean squared error of estimation, characterized by the slope, which is the ratio of the standard deviation of Y to the standard deviation of X, and the intercept, which is calculated from the average of Y minus the product of the slope and the average of X. An example is provided where the regression estimate predicts the number of characters in a chapter of 'Little Women' based on the number of periods.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So you have some positive residuals and some negative residuals. And we're going to see the... Mathematics magic behind why that's the case. So let's revisit correlation. So these are all reminders. So if you need to refresh your please go back and take a look at data aid or just calmly look at the slides after the lecture and it will all make sense. What was correlation? So I want to break down the definition of correlation here for you. The definition from data aid textbook says that correlation is the average of the product of X and Y. When both are measured in a standard unit. Right? Three pieces of information. And let me tell you what they are. It's starting from measuring in a standard unit. When we say that a variable is an standard unit, that means that it's zero center to center deviation of one. Right? So the average of all the values should be zero. And then the standard deviation of all of the values should be equal to one. And the way that we actually standardize a data is to go through this process right here, that you take one column of values or one set of values. You calculate their average, you subtract the average from each individual sample. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

leaf_chunk.audio: So you have some positive residuals and some negative residuals. And we're going to see the... Mathematics magic behind why that's the case. So let's revisit correlation. So these are all reminders. So if you need to refresh your please go back and take a look at data aid or just calmly look at the slides after the lecture and it will all make sense. What was correlation? So I want to break down the definition of correlation here for you. The definition from data aid textbook says that correlation is the average of the product of X and Y. When both are measured in a standard unit. Right? Three pieces of information. And let me tell you what they are. It's starting from measuring in a standard unit. When we say that a variable is an standard unit, that means that it's zero center to center deviation of one. Right? So the average of all the values should be zero. And then the standard deviation of all of the values should be equal to one. And the way that we actually standardize a data is to go through this process right here, that you take one column of values or one set of values. You calculate their average, you subtract the average from each individual sample.

children.title: Standardization and Pearson's Correlation Coefficient
children.summary: Standardization involves centering data by ensuring the mean is zero and adjusting the standard deviation to one by dividing by the standard deviation of the dataset. When discussing the product of two variables, X and Y, both in standard units, the average of these products is calculated by summing them and dividing by n, which is the number of observations. This average is known as Pearson's correlation coefficient, denoted as r, and is calculated using the formula that incorporates the standard deviations of X and Y.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's how you will make sure that it's zero centered, meaning that the mean is at zero. And then in order to make sure that the standard deviation is equal to one, you divide all of these transformations by standard deviation of that entire column or that set of values. This ensures that the standard deviation is equal to one as well. So that's what we mean by a standardization. So that's a standard unit. So when we say product of X and Y, both measured in a standard unit, this means that X measured in a standard unit is right here. Y measured in a standard unit is right here. So that's X and Y measured in a standard unit. And then there was an additional piece that says that the average of those products, and you're seeing that here I'm taking average by summing up all of these products and then dividing it by one over n, which is the average, right? children.title: Calculating Pearson's Correlation Coefficient
children.summary: A data set is described with two columns, x and y, containing n data points. The process involves zero centering or standardizing the values in each row, calculating the product of the standardized x and y values for each row, summing these products, and then averaging them by dividing by n. This process results in Pearson's correlation coefficient, which quantifies the correlation between the two variables.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what is i, what is n? And let's go back to our data set. So I'm assuming that in this data set, I have a data frame with two columns. I'm going to call one column column x, one column column y, and then the rows are row one, row two, and then all the way to row n. So assume that I have n data points. So this data set d has n data points with row one b x1 y1, row two b x2 y2, and then all the way to x and yn. So what I need to do is go through each one of those rows, zero center or standardize all of those values. Once all of those values are standardized, I will basically try to calculate the product of the two x standard unit, y standard unit in each given row, and then I have a value. And then all of those values need to be summed up together and then divided by, or multiplied by 1 over n to calculate the average of those products that were measured, that were x and y measured instead of unit. That's what it is. It's a complicated kind of equation, but once you remember what it was, it will make sense. This correlation is also called Pearson's coefficient or Pearson's correlation coefficient as well. So if you've heard multiple different terms for correlation, they all mean the correlation, and they all follow the same formula. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling
X

children.title: Covariance and Correlation in Data Analysis
children.summary: Covariance is calculated by removing the means of two variables, multiplying them, and averaging the result. It relates to correlation, which is defined as covariance divided by the product of the standard deviations of the two variables. Correlation indicates the degree of linear association between two variables, ranging from -1 to 1. A correlation close to 1 indicates a strong positive linear relationship, while a correlation close to -1 indicates a strong negative relationship. Correlation does not imply causation or indicate non-linear associations. When correlation is sufficiently high, simple linear regression can be applied, but if no linear relationship exists, regression modeling is not appropriate.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: If we don't want to standardize the data in a way that every column has a standard deviation of 1, But we want to make sure that there's zero centred, but they keep their standard deviation applying the similar formula is going to give us covariance. Covariance is basically meaning that remove the means, multiply them, and then pick the average. And this is the covariance. And covariance is equal to obviously based on the equation, is equal to correlation times the standard deviation of x times the standard deviation of y. These are repetitions from data aid just so that you know where that correlation was coming from when we were calculating this slope and kind of getting a sense of the fact that we needed to have correlation in order to figure out the relationship between X and X and Y. What we also know that is that the correlation shows us the linear association. It does not show us non-linear association. It does not show us causation in any sense. It just shows us the linear association or the degree of linear association between two variables x and y. children.title: Understanding Correlation and Linear Association
children.summary: A positive slope indicates a positive correlation, while a negative slope indicates a negative correlation, where an increase in x leads to a decrease in y. When correlation equals zero, it signifies a weak linear association, not a complete lack of association, and indicates that the two variables are uncorrelated. Correlation values range from -1 to 1, with the absolute value of r always less than 1. Examples illustrate cases of no clear correlation between x and y. Additionally, Pearson's correlation coefficient is defined as the average of the product of x and y in standard units.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's a positive slope, right? Negative correlation means that by negative is strong correlation means that by increasing x, your y is going to decrease. So that's a negative slope and that's why correlation that you have is going to control whether you're going to get a positive slope or a negative slope in your dataset or in your linear regression. When correlation is equal to zero, it shows that we have a weak linear association. It doesn't tell us that there is no association between them. It just says that the linear association is weak if you're concerned with linear association. And obviously I said that linear association does not mean causation. In any sense, it just means linear association for our purposes. When R is equal to zero, we would also say that the two variables are uncorrelated, meaning that there is no correlation between them. There is no reason that one increasing or decreasing one is going to have any influence or is going to show any influence on the other one. So there is no correlation between them. We would say that they're uncorrelated. correlation is a value between negative 1 and 1. So that's what I'm showing that the absolute value of r is always less than 1. And here are some examples. For example, in the first case, you're seeing that there is absolutely no clear sign that there is correlation or linear association between x and y. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

leaf_chunk.audio: So that's a positive slope, right? Negative correlation means that by negative is strong correlation means that by increasing x, your y is going to decrease. So that's a negative slope and that's why correlation that you have is going to control whether you're going to get a positive slope or a negative slope in your dataset or in your linear regression. When correlation is equal to zero, it shows that we have a weak linear association. It doesn't tell us that there is no association between them. It just says that the linear association is weak if you're concerned with linear association. And obviously I said that linear association does not mean causation. In any sense, it just means linear association for our purposes. When R is equal to zero, we would also say that the two variables are uncorrelated, meaning that there is no correlation between them. There is no reason that one increasing or decreasing one is going to have any influence or is going to show any influence on the other one. So there is no correlation between them. We would say that they're uncorrelated. correlation is a value between negative 1 and 1. So that's what I'm showing that the absolute value of r is always less than 1. And here are some examples. For example, in the first case, you're seeing that there is absolutely no clear sign that there is correlation or linear association between x and y.

children.title: Understanding Correlation and Pearson's Coefficient
children.summary: Correlation measures the strength of a linear association between two variables, represented by Pearson's correlation coefficient, denoted as r. The formula for calculating r involves the average of the product of x and y values, both measured in standard units. The correlation value ranges from -1 to 1, indicating the strength and direction of the relationship. A value close to 1 signifies a significant positive correlation, while values near 0 suggest a weak or no correlation. Additionally, covariance is mentioned as a related concept.

children.title: Correlation Analysis in Linear and Non-Linear Relationships
children.summary: As x increases, y tends to decrease, indicating a negative linear association with a correlation of -0.7. This is weaker compared to a previous case with a correlation of -0.95, attributed to data noise. In another scenario, a quadratic relationship exists between x and y, leading to a correlation close to zero, as there is no linear pattern despite a non-linear relationship. When discussing correlation in simple linear regression with zero-centered data, it's noted that the regression line passes through the origin, and a one-unit increase in x results in a corresponding increase in y.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: In the third case, what you're seeing is that as we're increasing x, the y decreases, right? Roughly y decreases. There's a little bit of noise obviously, but what we're expecting is that we're expecting to have some linear association, but the linear association is negative. And the valid digesting here is negative 0.7. And comparing negative points 7 with point 9.5 that we had in the second case, you were seeing that the linear association is this slightly weaker because we have some spread around some spread in the data or some noise in the data that is kind of keeping us from having a perfect linear association. But despite that, that issue we still see some linear association. In the last case, what would you guess the correlation be? The correlation, so this is kind of where I'm going to try to re-emphasize that what we're seeing in this case is that there is some relationship between x and y, right? What we're seeing in fact is a quadratic relationship. But since it's not a linear relationship, if you go ahead and calculate correlation, it's going to give us a correlation of close to zero. Because again, there is no linear pattern between x and y. But there is non-linear pattern that can be captured using other methods or other kinds of modeling that we can do. So moving on and kind of talking about what does it mean when we calculate correlation? When we have correlation of R in a simple linear regression data, simple linear regression word, and when our data is zero centered, meaning that when our data is zero centered, our regression line is going to pass through the origin. And then we talk about the correlation. That means that by every one unit increase in X, the Y value is going to increase by our unit. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

And comparing negative points 7 with point 9.5 that we had in the second case, you were seeing that the linear association is this slightly weaker because we have some spread around some spread in the data or some noise in the data that is kind of keeping us from having a perfect linear association. But despite that, that issue we still see some linear association. In the last case, what would you guess the correlation be? The correlation, so this is kind of where I'm going to try to re-emphasize that what we're seeing in this case is that there is some relationship between x and y, right? What we're seeing in fact is a quadratic relationship. But since it's not a linear relationship, if you go ahead and calculate correlation, it's going to give us a correlation of close to zero. Because again, there is no linear pattern between x and y. But there is non-linear pattern that can be captured using other methods or other kinds of modeling that we can do. So moving on and kind of talking about what does it mean when we calculate correlation? When we have correlation of R in a simple linear regression data, simple linear regression word, and when our data is zero centered, meaning that when our data is zero centered, our regression line is going to pass through the origin. And then we talk about the correlation. That means that by every one unit increase in X, the Y value is going to increase by our unit.

children.title: Understanding Regression Lines in Standard and Original Units
children.summary: A positive correlation coefficient (R) indicates an increasing trend, while a negative R indicates a decreasing trend, reflecting the slope of the regression line. The regression line in standard units has the equation y = r * x, passing through the origin. However, it is essential to interpret the regression line in the original units of the data. The equation in original units is expressed as y - average of y = r * (SD of y/SD of x) * (x - average of x), allowing for a more meaningful interpretation of the relationship between the variables.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And again, notice that if R is positive, that means that it's an increasing trend, when R is negative, that means that that's a decreasing trend, that's a negative slope, and that's how you can make sense of what's happening in the regression kind of line. But we don't want to live in the standard unit word. What we want to live in is in the original unit or the original kind of composition of the data set that we had. children.title: Restoring Data to Original Units in Regression
children.summary: Data points are adjusted back to their original units, causing the regression line to pass through the averages of x and y instead of the origin. This adjustment involves adding back the averages of x and y after previously centering the data. The scaling of y in relation to x is discussed, indicating that for every standard deviation unit change in x, y changes by the correlation coefficient r times the standard deviation of y. The equation for the regression line in non-standard units is presented, emphasizing the transformation from standard units to original units.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what we're going to do is we're going to move back all of our data all of our data sets or data points into the stand into the original units that they were meaning that our data point or our. A regression line is going to pass through average of x and average of y instead of passing through the origin. Right. Again, because we removed the, removed average of x from all of the x values and removed average of y from all the y values. When we added back, then it will basically push all of the data points by a factor of average of x and average of y. And that's why the origin is going to move to the point that corresponds to average of x and average of y. What you're seeing here is that x is the scale appropriately. Here we would say that for every standard deviation unit of x, y is going to be changed by r times the standard deviation of y. Right? That's how, again, you're scaling across the x, a scaling across the y because previously you divided by standard deviation of x and a standard deviation of y. If you multiply that back, this is what you're going to get. One is going to be multiplied by standard deviation of x, and R is going to be multiplied by standard deviation of y. That's why this scale is going to be different. So the equation that we will have, and this is the equation that you had seen in data 8 is that what we have is the equation for this line is y hat or the prediction for y minus y bar divided by a standard deviation of y. This is kind of moving all of these, all of the points or all of the equations from the top equation down to the bottom equation, but moving it to non-standard unit. children.title: Regression Line in Standard Units
children.summary: The segment discusses the regression line when variables x and y are measured in standard units. It explains the transformation of the predicted value y hat and how it relates to the average of y and the standard deviation of x. The equation y hat minus y bar equals r times the standard deviation of y multiplied by (x minus x bar divided by the standard deviation of x) is derived. The importance of isolating y hat and determining coefficients for the intercept and slope is emphasized, with a note that the slides will include animations to illustrate the steps.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So when I had y hat before, I'm going to here say that this y hat was measured in a standard unit, but the non-standard unit is going to be this y hat. So y hat minus average of y divided by standard division of x, I'm moving r down and then I'm also saying that the way that I calculated x in the standard unit was that x minus x bar divided by standard division of x. That's the equation that we'll be working with. If I move this equation to this address slide and try to simplify and just make sure that why hat remains on one side and then everything else is going to be remained on the right hand side and then I'm basically trying to get the coefficient, get a hat which is your intercept and also be hat which was your slope then we can basically figure out what is the the interest of equation and what is the, what is this little equation in our case. So let me start doing that. From this point and on, I might write equations on this slide, but when I publish this slide, the slides are going to have animations so you can actually walk through the animation and figure out all the steps. So what I'm going to do in order to simplify this equation is that I'm going to multiply both sides by standard deviation of y so that I can get the rate of the denominator in the left hand side. So what I'll have is y hat minus y bar is equal to r times the standard deviation of y multiplied by. x minus x bar divided by standard deviation of x. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

leaf_chunk.audio: So when I had y hat before, I'm going to here say that this y hat was measured in a standard unit, but the non-standard unit is going to be this y hat. So y hat minus average of y divided by standard division of x, I'm moving r down and then I'm also saying that the way that I calculated x in the standard unit was that x minus x bar divided by standard division of x. That's the equation that we'll be working with. If I move this equation to this address slide and try to simplify and just make sure that why hat remains on one side and then everything else is going to be remained on the right hand side and then I'm basically trying to get the coefficient, get a hat which is your intercept and also be hat which was your slope then we can basically figure out what is the the interest of equation and what is the, what is this little equation in our case. So let me start doing that. From this point and on, I might write equations on this slide, but when I publish this slide, the slides are going to have animations so you can actually walk through the animation and figure out all the steps. So what I'm going to do in order to simplify this equation is that I'm going to multiply both sides by standard deviation of y so that I can get the rate of the denominator in the left hand side. So what I'll have is y hat minus y bar is equal to r times the standard deviation of y multiplied by. x minus x bar divided by standard deviation of x.

children.title: Deriving the Regression Line Equation
children.summary: The equation for the predicted value, y hat, is derived by isolating terms related to x. The equation is rearranged to express y hat as a function of x, with coefficients separated. The slope, denoted as B hat, is identified as the product of the correlation coefficient r and the ratio of the standard deviations of y and x. This aligns with the previously established formula for the slope of the regression line.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Right. my goal is always to have this form, right? In order to get to that form, I just need to move also y bar to the other side of the equation. So what I'll have is that y hat is going to be equal to r times the standard deviation of y divided by a standard deviation of x multiplied by x minus x bar plus y bar. Right. So so far so good. The only thing that now I need to do is to isolate everything that is multiplied with x into one coefficient that I want to use as B hat, right? So I want to have one term that has all the things or all the coefficients that are multiplied with the variable and every constant term that's going to be added to this core term is going to be separate. So meaning that I want to actually split and take out x from this, parenthesis or this equation right here, which will tell me that why had this going to be equal to our times the standard deviation of y divided by standard deviation of x times x, right? And then the other thing that I'll have is Y bar. Minus are times the standard deviation of y divided by standard deviation of x times x bar. Right? I just rearranged some of the turns. So if we go ahead and take a look at what we had, the coefficient that's multiplied by x is going to be my slope or is going to be my b hat, which that coefficient right here is exactly what we're seeing right here. So this slope is going to be, I'm going to write it here, that this slope is going to be equal to R times the standard division of Y divided by standard division of X. This is what we had seen before. We just gave it to you in data 8 that this slope is equal to R times the standard division of Y divided by standard division of X. children.title: Regression Line Equation and Residuals
children.summary: The segment discusses the regression line equation, explaining the relationship between correlation, standard units, and the shape of the regression line. It details how the slope is determined, specifically stating that it is calculated as the change in Y for each standard unit increase in X, multiplied by the standard deviation of Y. The intercept is defined as y bar minus the slope times x bar. Additionally, it outlines the formula for the residual, which is the difference between the actual value and the predicted value (y hat), emphasizing the importance of distinguishing between exact and predicted values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: of x but this time we actually drive it for you. Brand the fact that What was correlation, what was the standard unit, and then how did we, what is the actual shape of the line that we have with every one standard unit, kind of increment in X, Y is going to be increased by our times the standard deviation of Y, and then technically that's how we arrived at this equation. Then the only other thing that I need to talk about is the intercept. Everything else, everything else that's remaining here is going to be intercept. I'm going to write that out that this is y bar minus. What you're noticing here is that this term right here is exactly what I had for this slope. So I can directly say that that term is equal to slope. So I have y bar minus slope. Times. Explore. And that was the equation for the slope that we had from before. That the slope is always y bar minus r times y bar minus a slope times x bar. And then obviously, what we have for the residual is that y residual or the error in prediction for data point i is equal to the exact value minus the predicted value y i hat. hat and I use hat because that's a predicted value and that's not the exact value that we calculate. children.title: Modeling Process and Regression Line Derivation
children.summary: The lecture focuses on deriving equations related to modeling using calculus and evaluating model effectiveness. The modeling process begins with the identification of a dataset. The regression line equation is introduced, detailing its components such as slope and intercept, and the error for each data point is defined as the difference between the actual and predicted values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then why I had you can actually derive it using this equation which now we derive both this lobe and interest of it we can directly have the equation of the line and actually go ahead and calculate it. So the goal that I have for today's lecture is actually go ahead and derive all of these equations one more time using calculus and then we'll see how we can actually do that. And what's the formal way of us defining how good of a model, how good a model can be and how we can objectively evaluate it. So let's go through the modeling process. So this is the process that we will go over every time that we want to build a model. Up to the end of the data 100 lecture, every time that I want to talk about creating a new model, I'm going to follow this same modeling process. And let me tell you what this modeling process involves. The first step of the modeling process is that every time that we start doing a modeling, there is going to be a data set. children.title: Introduction to Simple Linear Regression
children.summary: The data set consists of attributes or inputs used for predictions, with a target variable Y that the model aims to match. Each row in the data frame represents an observation containing features to model in simple linear regression, which focuses on a single input to predict a single target value. Predictions are denoted as y hats (y-i hat) for each observation. The discussion will transition to multiple regression in future lectures, which involves multiple features to predict the target variable.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Your data set is going to have one or more attributes or inputs that we're predicting, and then it's always going to have a Y or a target variable that we're trying to match it to. So there. Each row of our data frame that we're providing you is going to be an observation, which one piece of that is going to have inputs, features or attributes that we're going to model in the simple linear regression board. We're assuming that there is only one input that we're trying to match to the target. And then the second instance is always going to be output outcome or target variable. So we're trying to use X eyes in order to predict Y eyes as accurately as possible. Again, in simple linear regression, we're only concerned with having one feature or one input, and then we always have one target value. But when we would move to multiple regression, that's the case that we will have multiple different features, and we're trying to combine them together in order to create, in order to predict the output or target variable. But always the target variable is going to be a single value for every row of observation that we might have. Every time that we're referring to the fact that we're doing the prediction, we're going to say that the predictions are going to be y hats. For every row, there is going to be a y-i hat that we're predicting. Again, here I have n rows. So the predictions that I have is going to be y-i, y-1 hat, y-2 hats, and then so on and so forth until y-n hat. In terms of the models that we will build today and also in the next couple of lectures, we're going to start by building a simple linear regression. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

leaf_chunk.audio: Your data set is going to have one or more attributes or inputs that we're predicting, and then it's always going to have a Y or a target variable that we're trying to match it to. So there. Each row of our data frame that we're providing you is going to be an observation, which one piece of that is going to have inputs, features or attributes that we're going to model in the simple linear regression board. We're assuming that there is only one input that we're trying to match to the target. And then the second instance is always going to be output outcome or target variable. So we're trying to use X eyes in order to predict Y eyes as accurately as possible. Again, in simple linear regression, we're only concerned with having one feature or one input, and then we always have one target value. But when we would move to multiple regression, that's the case that we will have multiple different features, and we're trying to combine them together in order to create, in order to predict the output or target variable. But always the target variable is going to be a single value for every row of observation that we might have. Every time that we're referring to the fact that we're doing the prediction, we're going to say that the predictions are going to be y hats. For every row, there is going to be a y-i hat that we're predicting. Again, here I have n rows. So the predictions that I have is going to be y-i, y-1 hat, y-2 hats, and then so on and so forth until y-n hat. In terms of the models that we will build today and also in the next couple of lectures, we're going to start by building a simple linear regression.

children.title: Introduction to Parametric Models in Regression
children.summary: A simple linear regression is defined by an equation that includes an intercept term and a slope, represented as parameters (theta values). These models, including constant and multiple linear regression, are categorized as parametric models because they rely on parameters learned from data for estimation. The parameters, such as theta values, are distinct from the input features (x and y values). The optimal set of parameters, denoted as theta hats, is crucial for accurately modeling the dataset. The discussion emphasizes that defining a parametric model involves establishing a mathematical function.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, a simple linear regression is going as having equation like this, that there's always an intercept term, tetasera, and then there's always the slope that we're always referring to that as teta1 times input, and then we will combine the two terms in order to get the production. We will also see other types of models in terms of lecture and also next Tuesday that is going to be constant model and multiple linear regression that we'll talk about them later. I want to put out there that all the models that we'll be discussing, they're called parametric models. Because using the data, we will learn parameters of the model, all of those theta values are parameters that once we know those parameters and once we know what kind of model we use, then we can directly do the estimation of each one, or the calculation of each one of the estimated values as accurately as possible. So these are the parameters that we use for the modeling purposes and that's why there are parametric models. When we say that we have all of these parametric models, the only parameters that we have are all of those slow-band interest step and those coefficient values. Everything else is going to be input or the features that we're using. So in other words, the x and y values are not our parameters. So only the TETA values are our parameters. When I say that I use a parametric model that is parametrized by the TETA value? Then I will go ahead and drive what is the optimum set of TETA values that is going to be as accurately as possible model through my entire data set. We call them TETA hats. Hat on top of TETA means that there was a model that now this TETA parameter for that model is the optimum set of parameters. And I'm going to show you what that means if a model is not optimum, because technically when you define a model parameter, or when you're defining a parametric model, you're just defining a function or a mathematical function. leaf_chunk.audio: So, a simple linear regression is going as having equation like this, that there's always an intercept term, tetasera, and then there's always the slope that we're always referring to that as teta1 times input, and then we will combine the two terms in order to get the production. We will also see other types of models in terms of lecture and also next Tuesday that is going to be constant model and multiple linear regression that we'll talk about them later. I want to put out there that all the models that we'll be discussing, they're called parametric models. Because using the data, we will learn parameters of the model, all of those theta values are parameters that once we know those parameters and once we know what kind of model we use, then we can directly do the estimation of each one, or the calculation of each one of the estimated values as accurately as possible. So these are the parameters that we use for the modeling purposes and that's why there are parametric models. When we say that we have all of these parametric models, the only parameters that we have are all of those slow-band interest step and those coefficient values. Everything else is going to be input or the features that we're using. So in other words, the x and y values are not our parameters. So only the TETA values are our parameters. When I say that I use a parametric model that is parametrized by the TETA value? Then I will go ahead and drive what is the optimum set of TETA values that is going to be as accurately as possible model through my entire data set. We call them TETA hats. Hat on top of TETA means that there was a model that now this TETA parameter for that model is the optimum set of parameters. And I'm going to show you what that means if a model is not optimum, because technically when you define a model parameter, or when you're defining a parametric model, you're just defining a function or a mathematical function.

children.title: Model Parameters and Linear Regression Notation
children.summary: In modeling, the focus is on defining a family of functions to represent relationships in data rather than finding a single optimum function. The model equation is expressed as y hat = theta 0 + theta 1 * x, where theta represents model parameters. The optimal values of these parameters, denoted as theta hat, yield the best predictions across the dataset. The notation shifts from A and B to theta values to facilitate the inclusion of multiple features, allowing each feature to have an associated coefficient. This approach enables the representation of all parameters in a vector form, simplifying model communication and usage.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You're not trying to find out what is the optimum mathematical function that models your entire dataset. So this is what it means. When we have a model, we're basically trying to have, could define the family of functions that can be used in order to model our relationship. We would say that, okay, if I have the model parameters to be theta, then my model equation is going to be y hat equal to teta 0 plus teta 1 times x. When I say that I have the best optimum teta value, I will go ahead and put a hat on top of it, which basically means that this time, if you go ahead and calculate y hat values using these optimum values, this is the best prediction that you can have an average on the entire data set that we have. Again, this right here is going to be a family of the models that can be applied to our data set, but there is only one set of values that is tetA0 hadn't tetA1 had that are going to be optimal. Okay, and that's that that's where what we're going to be after. In data 100, one thing that you notice is that we're moving away from using A and B for linear regression and we're moving into the TETA 0 and TETA 1 ward. And that's very important. We're not using TETA 0 and TETA 1 because we think we're fancy, but this is because what I can do is that if I keep adding features or attribute to my model, then what I can have is that I can have a coefficient or the theta value associated with each one of those attributes, and I can combine all of them into a vector. So for example, in this case, what I've done is that I'm saying that my model uses theta zero and theta one. Let's combine them into a vector of theta. And you pass me theta or you pass me theta hat. I know what model you're using. I know what parameters you're using, and I can go ahead and use those parameters, right? Instead of just go to a, b, c, d, and so on and so forth. leaf_chunk.audio: You're not trying to find out what is the optimum mathematical function that models your entire dataset. So this is what it means. When we have a model, we're basically trying to have, could define the family of functions that can be used in order to model our relationship. We would say that, okay, if I have the model parameters to be theta, then my model equation is going to be y hat equal to teta 0 plus teta 1 times x. When I say that I have the best optimum teta value, I will go ahead and put a hat on top of it, which basically means that this time, if you go ahead and calculate y hat values using these optimum values, this is the best prediction that you can have an average on the entire data set that we have. Again, this right here is going to be a family of the models that can be applied to our data set, but there is only one set of values that is tetA0 hadn't tetA1 had that are going to be optimal. Okay, and that's that that's where what we're going to be after. In data 100, one thing that you notice is that we're moving away from using A and B for linear regression and we're moving into the TETA 0 and TETA 1 ward. And that's very important. We're not using TETA 0 and TETA 1 because we think we're fancy, but this is because what I can do is that if I keep adding features or attribute to my model, then what I can have is that I can have a coefficient or the theta value associated with each one of those attributes, and I can combine all of them into a vector. So for example, in this case, what I've done is that I'm saying that my model uses theta zero and theta one. Let's combine them into a vector of theta. And you pass me theta or you pass me theta hat. I know what model you're using. I know what parameters you're using, and I can go ahead and use those parameters, right? Instead of just go to a, b, c, d, and so on and so forth.

children.title: Parametric Models and Parameter Estimation
children.summary: Parametric models are defined by a limited number of parameters, such as 00 and 01, which are informed by the data rather than being predefined. The x and y values are not considered parameters since they are directly observed. The sample-based estimate of a parameter is denoted with a 'hat', which distinguishes it from the 'hat' used in predictions. The selection of parameters typically aims for the 'best' fit according to a chosen criterion. It is also noted that not all statistical models are parametric; for example, Kernel Density Estimations (KDEs) and k-Nearest Neighbor classifiers are classified as non-parametric models.

children.title: Parametric vs Non-parametric Models Overview
children.summary: The segment discusses the distinction between parametric and non-parametric models in statistics. It highlights that non-parametric models, such as Kernel Density Estimates (KDE) and K-nearest neighbors, do not require parameter settings, while parametric models do. The focus will be on learning and deriving parameters for parametric models, using simple linear regression as an example for the current lecture. The importance of selecting the appropriate model category for analysis is emphasized.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's kind of the notation that we'll be using. I said here we have a parametric model. We have seen non parametric models before. So some statistical models do not have parameters. For example, we have seen KDE's. KDE's were models, or kernel density estimate. But we did not set any parameters. We would just estimate alpha, and then we would just run a process, and we would have KDE's. Kairi's neighbors is also another model that you did not need to set parameters, or there was not no parameter to learn. So those are the cases that there are models that are non-parametric, there are models that are parametric, but we're dealing with is parametric models. And what we're going to do throughout this multiple lecture is to learn those parameters, how to optimally learn those parameters, how to derive those parameters so that we can uniquely have our own model to work with. So the terminology and we know the notation that we'll be using, let me walk you through the modeling process. Every time that we want to build a model, irrespective of what model you're building, you first need to choose the category of model that you want to use. So for example, for today's lecture, what we would say is that our model is going to be a simple linear regression. We might change our mind in the next few lectures, and we will keep using different types of models. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Modeling Process: Steps and Components
children.summary: The modeling process begins with choosing a model to represent the world, followed by selecting a loss function to quantify prediction error. The lecture will define loss functions and guide on how to choose one. After selecting the model and loss function, an optimization process will determine the best parameters for the model, referred to as TETA hats. Evaluation criteria will then be discussed to assess the model's performance, with simple linear regression serving as the model for introducing new terminology and notation.

children.title: Steps in the Modeling Process
children.summary: The first step in the modeling process is to choose a model, which involves determining how to represent the world. The second step is to select a loss function, which is used to quantify prediction error.

children.title: Choosing a Model and Loss Function
children.summary: The second step in the modeling process is selecting a loss function, which quantifies the accuracy of predictions. The lecture will define loss functions and discuss how to choose one in subsequent slides. Following the selection of the model and loss function, an optimization process will be conducted to determine the best parameters for the model, denoted as TETA hats. After fitting the model, evaluation criteria will be discussed to assess the model's performance on the dataset. For this lecture, simple linear regression will be used as the model to introduce new terminology and notation.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The second step is to choose a loss function. What is a loss function? Loss function is a function that will tell you how good your bad your predictions are. And I'm going to objectively define what this loss function means and how to choose a loss function in the next few slides. Once we have the model and we have the loss function, we'll go through an optimization process. This is a fun part that we'll actually go ahead and try to optimize our loss function in a way that we find the best set of parameters for our model. So we go from having TETA up to having TETA hats. And once we have TETA hats, we have our model, we have optimized model, and we're ready to go. Once we have our TETA hat and we fit the model and we find the optimum parameters, we need to evaluate the model. So we're going to briefly talk about whether the evaluation criteria that we'll have after we have the model to make sure that our model is performing well on the data set that we have. So let's start with choosing a model, which should be simple in this case. We're choosing simple linear regression for the purpose of today's lecture, because today's lecture is just basically setting you up with all the new notation and the new terminology that we're using. We're going from AMB, we're going into the word of TETA, so we're using TETA 0 as my Y interest of and TETA 1 as this loop that we're using. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling X

children.title: Simple Linear Regression and Parameter Estimation
children.summary: The segment discusses the theta vector in simple linear regression (SLR) for optimizing parameters to improve predictions. It highlights the use of hat notation to denote estimations, with a focus on theta representing optimal parameters. The basic formula for SLR is presented as y hat = theta 0 + theta 1 times x, clarifying that x is an input variable, not a parameter.

children.title: Understanding Simple Linear Regression and Parameter Estimation
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of a theta vector in the context of finding a family of functions for predictions in simple linear regression (SLR). It explains that the model aims to optimize parameters to achieve the best estimations. The use of a hat notation indicates estimations or predictions, with specific emphasis on how theta represents the optimal parameters. The segment concludes by outlining the basic formula for SLR, y hat = theta 0 + theta 1 times x, clarifying that x is an input, not a parameter.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what I said was that this tetah vector that we have is the finding a family of functions. So what that means is that if you are saying that you have a tetah vector and you pass the values of tetasurant, tetah one of that tetah vector, you pass the input, you have the predictions. So think about that as a machine that throughout the modeling process will try to find the parameters for this machine that gives us the best estimations, right? So that's what it means. What we have is that the parameters are sitting in the middle and we're trying to find the optimums that are parameters in the middle to get the best estimations, the best estimations as much as possible. So one thing to notice here is that I'm using also hat on top of the estimations. And I was using hat one more time when I was putting it on top of tetas. This is kind of operate or overloading that I'm using. So when it happens on top of why that means that's an estimation. When hat is on top of, or that's the prediction. When hat is on top of the teta, that means that that's the optimum set of parameters for the model that I'm using. The first step is done. We have a linear, simple linear regression. We know that the pattern of the function is going to be that two parameters are going to be given, and then we can calculate the prediction of y, which is y hat, using teta 0 plus theta 1 times x.
