children.title: Defining Loss Function and Model Accuracy
children.summary: The discussion defines the loss function and its importance in quantifying model accuracy against target values. It highlights the optimization of the cost function to find optimal parameters, denoted as theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat. A visual example illustrates three lines representing different theta values, with the blue line identified as the most accurate fit for the data set.

children.title: Defining Loss Function and Model Accuracy
children.summary: The discussion focuses on defining the loss function and its role in quantifying the accuracy of different models in relation to target values. It emphasizes the process of optimizing the cost function to determine the optimal parameters, referred to as theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat. A visual example is presented with three lines representing different theta values, prompting an evaluation of which line best fits the data set. The blue line is identified as the most accurate representation based on its alignment with the data.

So what we're wondering is that just looking at the shape of these lines and the distribution of the data, which one of these models are more accurate. children.title: Understanding Loss Functions and Cost Minimization
children.summary: The segment discusses loss functions in data modeling, emphasizing their role in measuring prediction accuracy by quantifying the error between predicted values (y hat) and actual values (y). A lower loss indicates better predictions, while a higher loss signifies greater discrepancies. The choice of loss function is crucial for model performance, with different functions suited for classification versus regression tasks. Specialized loss functions may be required for sensitive tasks like cancer identification to minimize missed cases. It covers L2 loss (squared loss), which penalizes larger errors more heavily, and L1 loss (absolute loss), which applies a consistent penalty regardless of error size, both commonly used in predictive modeling, particularly in linear regression. Additionally, the discussion on parametric functions highlights how they are parametrized by theta, with different theta values producing various predicted values (y-hats) that influence losses and the overall cost function. The goal is to identify theta values that minimize the average loss across all data points, leading to the smallest average residuals.

children.title: Understanding Loss Functions in Data Modeling
children.summary: The segment covers the concept of loss functions in data modeling, highlighting their role in measuring the accuracy of predictions by quantifying the error between predicted values (y hat) and actual values (y). A lower loss indicates better predictions, while a higher loss signifies greater discrepancies. The choice of loss function is crucial as it can significantly impact model performance, with different functions suited for tasks like classification versus regression. Specialized loss functions may be necessary for sensitive tasks, such as cancer identification, to minimize the risk of missing cases. The discussion includes L2 loss (squared loss), which penalizes larger errors more heavily, and L1 loss (absolute loss), which applies a consistent penalty regardless of error size. Both loss functions are commonly used in predictive modeling, particularly in linear regression.

children.title: Understanding Loss Functions in Data Modeling
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of loss functions in data modeling, emphasizing the importance of measuring the distance between predicted values (y hat) and actual values (y). It explains that a lower loss indicates better predictions, while a higher loss signifies greater discrepancies. The choice of loss function significantly impacts model performance, and the course will cover three to four different types of loss functions that serve similar purposes.

children.title: Understanding Loss Functions in Modeling
children.summary: Loss functions measure the accuracy of predictions by quantifying the error between predicted values and actual outcomes. They penalize predictions based on their proximity to actual values, with greater penalties for larger discrepancies. The choice of loss function varies depending on the modeling task, such as classification versus regression. For instance, in sensitive tasks like cancer identification, specialized loss functions are necessary to minimize the risk of missing cases. The discussion will cover various loss functions, emphasizing that the selection depends on the specific use case and can significantly impact model accuracy and computational efficiency.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It will tell us how bad our predictions were, and it will basically tell us that it will penalize us more if our prediction is far from the actual value, and it will penalize us less if our prediction is close to the actual target value. But the choice of the loss function really depends on multiple different metrics and different types of modeling that you're doing. For example, if you're doing a classification task versus a regression task, the loss function is going to be very different. If you're doing a classification task, for example, if you're doing cancer identification in a sense, you really don't want to miss any potential cancer patients. So in those cases, you really want to have a different kinds of loss function that accounts for all of those cases as well. So we're going to take a look at all of those considerations when we go into evaluating the models a little bit more, or just putting it out there that when I talk about loss function today, I'm going to introduce two loss functions, but those are not the only loss functions that we use. Depending on the use case, the loss function can be different. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: L2 and L1 Loss Functions Explained
children.summary: The segment discusses two loss functions used in modeling: L2 loss (squared loss) and L1 loss (absolute loss). L2 loss calculates the residual by subtracting the predicted value from the actual value and then squaring it, which penalizes larger errors more heavily. For example, a residual of 100 results in a penalty of 10,000. In contrast, L1 loss takes the absolute value of the residual, meaning that a residual of 100 remains as 100, applying a consistent penalty regardless of the size of the error. Both loss functions are widely used in predictive modeling.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, last function of the day, you might have seen these equations before. One last function is called L2 loss or a squirt loss. One loss is called absolute loss or L1 loss, order 1, order 2. Right? Think about them like that. So the squirt loss basically says that go ahead and take a look at the actual value, subtract the predicted value, so meaning that calculate the residual, and go ahead and score the residual. So anytime that you saw an error, a score that error, which basically means that if your error is very a small value, I'm scoring it and I'm even making it smaller, right? So if you're taking a look at the residual being a value between zero and one and you score it, that's even going to get a smaller. So it's not going to be penalized as much. Compared to the case that, okay, If you have 100 units of residual, it will square it to penalize you even more. So the penalty right now is going to be 10,000 in that case. So that's kind of L2 loss or the second order loss that we have. The absolute loss just looks at the residual and calculus the absolute value. Again, meaning that if it's the smallest, it's just going to be that same absolute value of the residual. If it's 100 unit of residual, it's just going to be same 100 units. children.title: L2 and L1 Loss in Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the concepts of L2 and L1 loss in the context of linear regression. It emphasizes that loss is always a positive value, representing a cost. The speaker explains how to express the loss for simple linear regression using the equations for L2 (squared loss) and L1 (absolute loss), highlighting the importance of matching the line to all data points in the dataset. The average loss across all data points is considered to achieve a better fit.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's not going to be penalized any further. But in both of the cases, they're penalizing. And in both of the cases, what you notice is that the loss is always a positive value because it's a cost. So we want to give a cost. We don't want to give a negative cost. So in the cost in both of the cases, in our case, is positive. So what does it mean in the... in the language of linear regression, that means that I'm going to go ahead and replace in every instance that I have y hat, I'm going to replace it with the equation for the simple linear regression. So here I have y minus y hat, so here my loss for simple linear regression based on L2 or a squared loss is going to be y minus this right here is y hat and then the squared. Same thing for the L1 loss, we just plug in y hat and then we have the equation. Note to step what I have right here is the loss or the cost or a single data point. But what we want is we want to have a line that matches all of the data sets, all of the data points in the data set as closely as possible. So in that case, what we would do is we would go ahead and take average over all of the data points. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

And in both of the cases, what you notice is that the loss is always a positive value because it's a cost. So we want to give a cost. We don't want to give a negative cost. So in the cost in both of the cases, in our case, is positive. So what does it mean in the... in the language of linear regression, that means that I'm going to go ahead and replace in every instance that I have y hat, I'm going to replace it with the equation for the simple linear regression. So here I have y minus y hat, so here my loss for simple linear regression based on L2 or a squared loss is going to be y minus this right here is y hat and then the squared. Same thing for the L1 loss, we just plug in y hat and then we have the equation. Note to step what I have right here is the loss or the cost or a single data point. But what we want is we want to have a line that matches all of the data sets, all of the data points in the data set as closely as possible. So in that case, what we would do is we would go ahead and take average over all of the data points.

children.title: Comparison of L1 and L2 Loss Functions
children.summary: Discussion on the ideal loss function for penalizing large residuals more than small ones, highlighting that squared loss is preferable for this purpose. It addresses the drawback of using squared loss, particularly in relation to outliers, which can skew the model away from the actual data trend. The segment also raises the question of why residual values are scored and why absolute values are used to ensure all cost values are positive, preventing the sum from equaling zero.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So let's go through two more slider questions to make sure that we're all on the same page with respect to What is L1 loss, what is L2 loss and so on and so forth? So the question is if you want to penalize large residuals more than the small residuals or proportionally, which loss function is more ideal? Do you want to use absolute loss or do you want to use the squared loss? looks like a squirt loss is the winner because of the example that I gave right so if you really want to penalize large miscalculations or misperdictions you really want to use the squirt loss in those cases what we've been a drawback if we penalize large deviation so much There is one drawback in that case. Just a quick hint would be an outlier. Anything about outliers might be an issue here. Yeah. So the answer was that the outliers are generally far from the trend of the data. And the model, if model wants to try to avoid having that squared large loss that it might incur, it might tilt the line or the model from the actual trend that it needs to be to the trend that's kind of have meets halfway in between. So that it gets some small loss on the actual trend that the data and also gets closer to the outlier data point that it has. What about this question? Why don't we use the actual residual values directly? Why do we score them? Why do we calculate absolute value? Why do we want all of them to be all of the cost values to be positive? Why do we want to get rid of negatives? Sum will be zero. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: Calculating Loss in Model Training
children.summary: Residual values can be both positive and negative, leading to cancellation when summed, which is problematic for cost calculation. To ensure all values are positive, absolute loss or squared loss is used. The total loss for all data points is calculated by summing the loss values and dividing by the number of data points (n) to obtain the average. This approach allows for fair comparison of model performance across different data sets without the influence of data set size.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Residual sum to zero, that is correct. Um... Okay, they don't cancel out. So that seems to be the right trend. But the issue that we have when we try to just use the actual residual values is that some residual values are positive, some residual values are negative, right? This is what we saw before. And if we just actually get the residual values and sum them up, the issue is going to be there, going to cancel each other out. And again, we need to calculate the cost and aggregate in the entire data sets. So that's why we really want to make sure that all of them are positive cost values. And that's why we're using either absolute loss or a squared loss in all of these cases. I said we need to make sure that we calculate the loss for all of the data points. In fact, the way that we do it is that we will go ahead and sum up all of these loss values for all of the data points. So you see why I, why I have. That means that the prediction for data point I and assuming that we have n different data points, we will sum up all of those loss values that we have and then we will divide it by n, which is going to be calculating the average. Right? Summing up makes sense because I want to take a look at the entire data set. When I'm dividing it by n, this is the case that if I'm comparing the model performances on different data sets, I don't want the sides of the data sets to be influencing my decision about which model is performing better, right? Or which model is performing? We'll object the function that we'll be using in order to train a model and find the optimum set of parameters that we have. Video: 1: v

children.title: Parametric Functions and Cost Minimization
children.summary: The discussion covers parametric functions, focusing on how they are parametrized by theta. Different theta values produce various predicted values (y-hats), which influence the losses for data points and the overall cost function. The objective is to identify theta values that minimize the average loss across all data points, resulting in the smallest average residuals. The relationship between theta and the model is highlighted, indicating that changes in theta affect predictions and the cost function.

children.title: Parametric Functions and Cost Minimization
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the concept of parametric functions, specifically how the function is parametrized by theta. Different theta values yield different predicted values (y-hats), which in turn affect the losses for data points and the overall cost function. The goal is to find theta values that minimize the average loss across all data points, leading to the smallest average residuals. The relationship between theta and the model is emphasized, as changing theta alters the predictions and consequently the cost function.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: One note is that what you notice is that this function is a parametric function. It's parametrized by theta, meaning that if you give me different theta values here, I'm going to give you different y-hats. And by having different y-hats, you're going to have different losses for the data points. And then once you have different losses, you're going to have a different overall cost or the overall average loss. So, kind of notice that this function, depending on the teta is going to change, and our goal in this case is going to be to find the teta value or the set of teta zero and teta one values that minimizes this cost function that goes through all of the data points as closely as possible on average and have the minimum average residual values that we have. Okay, does that make sense? So this cost function is a really important cost function. Again, it's parametrized by TETA, meaning that depending on the TETA value, the value of the cost on the data set is going to change. And why is it going to change? Because when you change the TETA value, you're effectively changing the model. When you're changing the model, your cost function is going to be different because your predictions are going to be different. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling X

children.title: L2 and L1 Loss Functions; Partial Derivatives in ML
children.summary: The segment covers L2 loss (mean squared error, MSE) and L1 loss (mean absolute error, MAE) in regression, explaining that L2 loss is calculated as the average of squared differences between actual and predicted values. It introduces least squares regression, emphasizing the goal of minimizing error scores. The role of partial derivatives in machine learning is highlighted, particularly for minimizing MSE in simple linear regression, where derivatives are set to zero to find optimal parameter values. The calculation of MSE and its derivative with respect to model parameters is elaborated, treating all other variables as constants. Additionally, the derivative of summation terms is discussed, noting that the derivative of a sum equals the sum of the derivatives, resulting in an expression involving the average of the derivatives.

children.title: L2 and L1 Loss Functions; Partial Derivatives in ML
children.summary: The segment covers L2 loss (mean squared error, MSE) and L1 loss (mean absolute error, MAE) in regression, detailing how L2 loss is calculated as the average of squared differences between actual and predicted values. It introduces least squares regression, focusing on minimizing error scores. The importance of partial derivatives in machine learning is discussed, particularly in minimizing MSE for simple linear regression, where derivatives are set to zero to find optimal values. The calculation of MSE and its derivative with respect to model parameters is explained, with emphasis on treating all variables except the parameter of interest as constants. Finally, the derivative of summation terms is addressed, noting that the derivative of a sum equals the sum of the derivatives, leading to an expression involving the average of the derivatives.

children.title: L2 and L1 Loss Functions in Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the evaluation of loss functions in relation to a dataset, specifically focusing on L2 loss (mean squared error, MSE) and L1 loss (mean absolute error, MAE). It explains that the empirical risk for L2 loss is calculated as the average of the squared differences between actual and predicted values. The speaker plans to demonstrate the differences in final models obtained using these loss functions in future lectures. The terminology of least squares regression is introduced, emphasizing the goal of minimizing error scores across the dataset, linking it to simple linear regression.

And if you plug in L1 loss instead of this loss function into the empirical risk equation, this is going to give us 1 over N times the summation of absolute value of all of those residual values that we have. So, MSc versus MAE. In the next lecture, I'm going to come back and play with optimizing both of these functions. And I'm going to show you what's the difference between the final model that we'll get depending on which loss function that we use and which empirical response we get at the end. For today's purpose, what I'm going to do is I'm going to say that I'm going to use L2 loss, meaning that I'm going to go ahead and calculate the residual square of each one of those data points in the dataset. And my entire empirical risk function is going to be MSE or mean squared error. Again, this is the mean and this is the squared error of all of those predictions that we have. This is YI, this is YI hat that we have based on the simple linear regression. What you know from data 8 is that you know that they called this delinear regression that they were teaching you, they called it least the squares regression. We also used exactly the same terminology here. Effectively, simple linear regression pattern or function together with the optimization function that is mean squared error is called least the scores regression because we're trying to find a function that minimizes these scores of the errors that we have over the entire population. So now down to the fun part. I said that if you have the two parts in the model, you have the model kind of Hadron or the template which in this case is simple linear regression and you know what kind of cost function you're using Then everything else is going to be easy fitting the model is going to be easy What I'll do is I'll basically tell you that what does it mean to fit the model when we say that we want to fit the model That means that I want to find a combination of theta 0 and theta 1 values that are giving me the minimum function or the minimum value for the right hand side of this msc function So in order to do so, what you know from your math courses is that I need to calculate the derivative of this function with respect to theta 0, set it to 0 with respect to theta 1, set it equal to 0, find those optimum points, and those optimum points, theta 0 and theta 1 are going to be exactly my theta 0 had on my theta 1 hat that I can use.

children.title: Introduction to Partial Derivatives in Machine Learning
children.summary: The segment introduces the concept of partial derivatives, emphasizing their importance in machine learning and the application of calculus and linear algebra. It discusses the process of minimizing the Mean Squared Error (MSE) for a simple linear regression model, highlighting the need to set derivatives equal to zero to find optimal values. The equation for MSE is presented, along with the partial derivatives with respect to the regression coefficients.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the next few kind of or five or so minutes is going to be me driving all of these partial derivatives for you. It might sound scary, but this is kind of the first starting point for you to get comfortable with this entire pipeline. And actually appreciate that all of the math courses that you learned so far is actually going to come in use in machine learning. Because Machine learning is all about having various strong calculus and linear algebra background and this is this is the place that you will see where it will become handy. children.title: Calculating Mean Squared Error Derivative
children.summary: The segment discusses the calculation of the mean squared error (MSE) and its derivative with respect to the model parameters. The MSE is defined as the average of the squared residuals, represented as the difference between the actual values (y_i) and the predicted values (y_i hat). The speaker explains that while taking the derivative, all variables except for the parameter of interest (tetasera) are treated as constants.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's on. Okay cool. So, I have the objective function and let me reiterate what the subjective function was. Remember that the subjective function is calculating the average. over the scores of the errors. Right? So this is the error inside the parentheses. What you're seeing is y i minus y i hat, which is the residual to the power of two that I'm calculating, which is mean a scored error that I want to calculate. So I'm going to take the derivative with respect to tetasera, which effectively what that means is that I'll do the normal derivative, but I'm just assuming that anything except for tetasera is going to be constant value, right? children.title: Taking Derivative of Summation Terms
children.summary: The derivative of a summation term is discussed, emphasizing that the derivative of a sum is equal to the sum of the derivatives. The coefficient and summation are moved out of the derivatives for clarity, leading to an expression of 1/n times the summation of the derivatives of the term (yi - theta zero). This is part of calculating the partial derivative of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to parameters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And there are a couple of steps that I need to do first. One is that I'm taking the derivative of a summation term, right? And we know that the derivative of sum is equal to sum of the derivatives. So I'm going to take out the coefficient and the summation out of the derivatives so that things are a little bit easier and cleaner. So moving this summation out is is going to give me 1 over n times the summation of pi from 1 up to n of the derivative of what I had, which is yi minus theta zero. children.title: Partial Derivative of MSE with Respect to Parameters
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of taking the partial derivative of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to the parameters theta_0 and theta_1. It begins by applying the chain rule to differentiate the summation, which involves recognizing that the derivative of the summation equals the sum of the derivatives. The outer function is identified as the squared term, leading to a factor of two. The inner function, which is the expression involving yi, theta_0, and theta_1, is differentiated, yielding a coefficient of negative one for the derivative of negative theta_0. The final expression simplifies to negative 2/n times the summation of the differences between yi and the linear model prediction.

I'm taking some of those derivatives. to take a derivative with respect to teta 0, teta 0 inside is raised to the power of 2. So you know that in this case, I need to apply chain rule first, meaning that I basically try to take the derivative of the outer function and then take the derivative of the inner function, which in this case is going to be meary writing, the coefficient right here, 1 over n times the summation of i from 1 up to n up. function times the derivative of function. Right? If you think about this as function to the power of two. So what I'll do is I'll say that two times the function which is equal to yi minus is that the zero minus that the one times x i. And then what is the derivative of this function with respect to ? 0 ? So again assuming that everything except for tetasero is a constant term. children.title: Partial Derivative of MSE with Respect to Theta Zero and One
children.summary: The segment discusses calculating the partial derivative of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to the parameters theta zero and theta one. It outlines the formula for MSE, which is the sum of the squared differences between the actual values and the predicted values, and emphasizes the use of the chain rule in deriving the derivatives. The process involves summing the derivatives across all data points to simplify the expression.

children.title: Partial Derivative of MSE Calculation
children.summary: The process of calculating the partial derivative of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to parameters theta 0 and theta 1 is discussed. The derivative of the MSE, expressed as MSE = (1/n) * Σ(yi - (theta 0 + theta 1 * Xi))^2, is derived by applying the chain rule. The speaker emphasizes that when taking the derivative, theta 1 is treated as the variable while other components are considered constants. The derivative results in a formula involving the original function and its derivative, leading to a simplification step.

So it's going to give me 1 over n times the summation of i from 1 up to n of. Um... of the derivative of. Yes. Why I minus. That is 0 minus Teta 1 Xi is square with respect to Teta 1. Right. children.title: Applying Chain Rule to MSE Derivative
children.summary: The chain rule is applied to find the derivative of the Mean Squared Error (MSE) with respect to parameters theta zero and theta one. The expression involves the summation of the differences between observed values (y_i) and the predicted values (theta zero + theta one * x_i). The derivative with respect to theta one is identified as negative x_i. Further simplifications of constants and terms in the derivative process are discussed.

children.title: Derivation of Residuals and Optimal Parameters
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the derivation of equations related to residuals in a regression context. It explains that when the summation of all residuals is set to zero, it indicates that the optimum value for theta zero (θ0) has been reached. Additionally, the right-hand side of the equation shows that the summation of residuals multiplied by the corresponding x values (xi) should also equal zero. The segment concludes with the identification of two equations with two unknowns, θ0 and θ1, which can be solved to find the optimal values θ0 hat and θ1 hat.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So it's two times f times whatever. So this is going to be negative 2 over n of the summation of i from 1 up to n of yi minus minus theta zero minus theta one x i times x i. Right. Look at both sides. Look at that function f thing that's in common between them. So one is effectively telling me that if I go ahead and set this to be equal to zero, one, the left hand side is effectively telling me that the summation of all of the residuals should be equal to zero. Because that's residual. And that's why they were canceling each other out. That's why they couldn't be a cost function. Right? This is telling me that at the point that theta zero is optimum, the summation of residuals should be equal to zero. And then the right hand side is basically telling me that The summation of residuals times x i's should be equal to zero. Right, we're going to get to that. And what I need to do right now is that I have two equations and I have two unknowns. And whether the unknowns that I have, so I have two equations, there is one unknown, teta zero and there is one unknown, teta one. I said both of them equal to zero and then I solve it for the optimum teta zero and optimum teta one and that will give me teta zero hat and teta one hat, which are the optimum values that we want. children.title: Estimating Equations and Derivatives for MSE
children.summary: The segment discusses the estimating equations used to find optimum theta values, specifically focusing on the summation of residuals equating to zero. It presents the equations for the mean squared error (MSE) and begins deriving the first step to simplify the equation. The process involves applying derivatives and the chain rule to the MSE formula, leading to further simplifications necessary for estimating the parameters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Again, you'll get this slide with animation and fancy, animation, so you will get a clean version of all of these. So this is where we left off. So remember, that was the equation from the left hand side. The second row is the equation from the right hand side. So the summation of residuals is equal to 0. Summation of residuals times xi equal to 0. What I need to do is I need to just go ahead and use these equations that I'm calling them estimating equations because I'm using them in order to estimate optimum theta i values in order to, I'll solve them in order to find out those optimum values exactly. So let's go ahead and do that. So these are the two equations. One over n times the summation of yi minus yi, i hat is equal to zero. And then the second equation is on the right hand side. What I'll do is I'll derive the first step. So deriving the first step means that what does it mean for this equation to be equal to zero? Is there any way that we can simplify it further? children.title: Estimating Equations and Their Solutions
children.summary: The segment discusses the formulation of estimating equations to determine parameters theta_0 and theta_1. It describes the process of breaking down a summation into separate terms, specifically focusing on the equation represented as 1/n times the summation of i from 1 to n of y_i, followed by the subtraction of 1/n times the summation of i from 1 to n of theta_0 hat and 1/n times the summation of i from 1 to n of theta_1 hat x_i. The goal is to solve two estimating equations: the first being the mean of the residuals equated to zero, and the second involving the product of the residuals and the predictor variable.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The compose all of these tree terms that are inside the parentheses and inside the summation into separate terms. Meaning that what I'll do is I'll say that this equation is equal to 1 over n times the summation of i from 1 up to n of yi. That's the first term. And then I'll stay minus 1 over n times the summation of i from 1 up to n of teta 0 hat. Right? And then minus... 1 over n times this dimension of i from 1 up to n of theta 1 hat x i equal to 0. children.title: Optimal Values from Estimating Equations
children.summary: The segment discusses deriving optimal values for parameters theta 0 and theta 1 by setting their partial derivatives equal to zero, resulting in theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat. It emphasizes that these values represent the optimum parameters obtained from solving estimating equations. The discussion also refers back to previous slides for context and will later provide the actual values for these optimal parameters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Can someone tell me why my tetas right now turned into tetahats? Because it's optimized to zero. Remember in the previous slide when I was on the previous slide here. Actually, yeah, right here when I was driving all of these values, the tetah 0 and tetah 1, they were just normal tetah 0 and tetah 1 of the function. I actually derived it and calculated the partial derivative with respect to tetah 0, with respect to tetah 1. At the point that you actually set it equal to 0, which is what we have right here, I set this equal to 0, then tetah 0 will become tetah 0 hat, because that's the optimum theta 0. And theta 1 will become theta 1 hat, because I'm also setting this one equal to 0. So that's at the point that you're calculating the optimum and you're finding the exact optimum values by setting the partial derivatives equal to 0, that's when theta 0 and theta 1 will turn into theta 0 hat and theta 1 hat. Let me go back to this place that we were. So that's why I have theta 0 hat and theta one hat right here as I'm solving. And then at the end, we'll get to what is the actual value for theta zero hat and what is the actual value for theta one hat. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Simplifying Linear Regression Terms
children.summary: The segment discusses the simplification of terms in a linear regression context. It explains that the average of the y values, represented as one over n times the summation of yi values, simplifies to the average y value (y bar). The constant term, theta zero hat, when summed n times, also simplifies to theta zero hat. The last term involves pulling out theta one hat as a constant, leading to the expression theta one hat times the summation of xi values, which simplifies to theta one hat times x bar. This derivation reinforces the relationship theta zero hat equals y bar minus the slope times x bar, aligning with previous calculations in simple linear regression.

leaf_chunk.text:

The other thing, what is the simplified version of this? Teta 0 hat is a constant value. I'm summing it up n times, that this is going to be n times Teta 0 hat, and then I'm multiplying 1 over n. So this is equal to just teta zero hat. So the second term is teta zero hat. And then what is the last term? So the last term, there's no easy way to simplify the except for saying that I'm going to pull out theta one hat outside of the summation because it's a constant value. So then I'll have theta one hat times the summation of i from one up to n of x i's, which this one right here is also going to be x bar because that's the Sorry, there's one over n. One over n times the summation of x i's is going to be x bar. Again, setting it equal to zero, kind of cleaning up what I wrote here. y bar minus teta zero, hat minus. children.title: Finding Theta Estimates from Equations
children.summary: Partial derivatives were calculated and set to zero to determine the optimal theta_0 value, resulting in theta_0 hat being equal to y bar minus the slope times x bar. The focus then shifted to finding theta_1 hat using two equations, with an algebraic trick involving subtracting equation 1 multiplied by x bar from equation 2. The details of this derivation were outlined, emphasizing that the algebraic manipulation is not required for exams.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We calculated partial derivatives, we set partial derivatives to equal to zero in order to find out what is the optimum tetaheus zero valiant that's a one value. And then it just happens that if I simplify the first function or the first derivation, it's going to tell me that your theta 0 hat is going to be equal to y bar minus the slope times x bar, which is what we know from a slight tree of today's lecture. But now we systematically drive that and now we have a formal way of defining the objective function. The only thing that we need to now do is to figure out what is this theta 1 hat. But we still have two equations. We just looked at one of the equations. If we combine the two equations together, which I'm not going to try to derive it manually, but I'm going to walk you through the steps, is... It's going to help us to find out Teta 1 hat. The trick that we're going to use here is that we're going to take equation 2. We're going to subtract equation 1 times x bar from it. This is just an algebraic trick that you don't need to know and you should not be expected to know in the exams. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc

children.title: Deriving Theta Estimates in Regression
children.summary: The segment focuses on deriving the estimate for theta one hat in a regression context. It begins with the manipulation of equation 2, where the term involves the average of x (x bar) to align with the x i term in equation 1. The process includes factoring out the common term (y i - y i hat) and substituting y i hat with its equation, which is defined as theta zero plus theta one times x i. Additionally, theta zero is expressed in terms of y bar and the slope, leading to a consolidated equation for further analysis.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But this is what I'm using in order to derive how we can find the Teta 1 hat. So if I go ahead and do the calculations, So what you're seeing is that this is equation 2 right here that we're seeing. This term right here is the equation 1 multiplied with x bar or the average of x. You can kind of guess why I multiplied it with x bar. This is because the equation 2 has that x i term dangling there and it just need to have something of the nature of x for to accompany it. And that's exactly what I'm using it. So what I can do right now is going to be I'm factoring out all of the y i's minus y i hats because that's the term that's in common between them. So then this is how this is going to turn out to be y i minus y i hat and then x i minus x x bar and then multiplied some up average it's equal to zero. I'm going to take one more step. I'm going to substitute y, y, i hat here with the equation for y, i hat. So the optimum y i hat is going to be theta zero plus theta one times x i. So I'm going to use that. What I'm going to use again is going to be I'm going to replace theta zero with the equation that we just derived. Right? The equation for theta zero was that it's y bar minus slope, which is set to 1 hat times x bar. So now what you're seeing is that I have one equation. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling

children.title: Deriving Theta One Hat in Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the derivation of the estimator theta one hat (θ1̂) in a regression context. It explains that all variables are known except for θ1̂, which can be simplified from the equation. The left-hand side of the equation is shown to be equal to the product of the correlation coefficient (r) and the standard deviations of x and y. The derivation leads to the conclusion that θ1̂ is equal to r multiplied by the standard deviation of x divided by the standard deviation of y.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: All of the variables are known except for a teta one hat. And what I can do is I can just keep simplifying it in order to find out what is teta one hat from this equation. So if I keep simplifying, which I'm not going to go through the steps, it's going to give us this equation right here. So what is this equation? This equation right here is telling me that, uh, okay, so you have yi minus y bar, you have xi minus x bar, and the right hand side you have, teta 1 hat multiplied with the, with the variance of x. Um, so kind of a reminder from what we know from correlation, you know that the correlation was equal to this equation right here, which sounds similar to what we have in the left hand side. So what we can do is we can say that the left-hand side right now is going to be equal to r times a standard deviation of x times a standard deviation of y because the only thing that this this equation is missing compared to this equation is these denominators right here. So the left-hand side of this equation is r times a standard deviation of x times a standard deviation of y. The left-hand side is my theta one hat times the variance or a standard deviation in the square of x. And then what's happening here is that I only have one unknown. I can directly solve it. Now that the one hat is going to be equal to our times the standard division of x over a standard division of y. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Defining Machine Learning Modeling and Cost Functions
children.summary: Machine learning modeling involves defining a mathematical objective function rather than guessing. A family of functions or model pattern is established, exemplified by the equation for simple linear regression. The effectiveness of the function is evaluated using a cost function, specifically the mean squared error. Partial derivatives are calculated to find optimal values for parameters teta 0 and teta 1, resulting in teta 0 hat and teta 1 hat. Key points include that the average of residuals equals zero and the summation of residuals also equals zero, indicating that residuals are orthogonal to the predicted variables, which will be discussed in the next session.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This kind of sounds like magic, but now we formally define how actual machine learning people do modeling. They don't guess. They basically have a mathematical objective function that they define. They define the family of functions or the model pattern, which is what we did, but define what is the equation for simple linear regression. But we said that, okay, depending on the tetasero and tetabone value, it's going to be a different function or a different line that we're mapping. How do we find out how good that function is with defined the cost function? We actually use minus squared error, we actually venture calculating partial derivatives in order to find out, Find out the optimum values for teta 0 and teta 1, which got us the teta 0 hat and teta 1 hat that we have. So this is where we are. What we got at the end was that was the two kind of final notes that I told you when I was doing the derivation. I said that the average of residuals is equal to zero or the summation of residuals is equal to zero. And I also said that the residual multiplied with the x i is equal to zero. I think on Tuesday of next week, we're going to talk about the fact that this means that the residuals are orthogonal to the predicted variables. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modeling X

children.title: Model Evaluation Techniques in Data Analysis
children.summary: Model evaluation involves several key steps to assess performance. First, data visualization and statistical computation, including the correlation coefficient, are performed. If the correlation coefficient is acceptable, linear regression analysis follows. Additionally, the mean squared error is calculated, along with its square root, known as the root mean squared error. This conversion back to the original data units is crucial for meaningful estimation, as squaring the errors initially alters the units.

The first thing is that a lot of time we would go ahead and visualize the data and we will compute the statistics. Again, we will calculate the correlation coefficient if the correlation coefficient is acceptable then we do the linear regression analysis. The other way that we evaluate the performance of the model is that we will calculate mean a squared error and we will calculate the square root of that. That's called root minus the square root error. And the reason that we calculate the square root of it is because we want to turn the error into the original unit of the data. Because the error is in the second order. It's when we're calculating a square root of the errors, we were squaring the error. So the unit was in the squared units. So by taking a square root, we're going back to the original unit of the data. So that value is going to be meaningful if you're doing any estimation. Video: 1: Lec 10 - DS100 Fa24 - Modelinc X

children.title: Visualizing Residuals and Model Evaluation
children.summary: Visualizing residuals is crucial for assessing the fit of a regression model. When plotting residuals against x-values, the ideal outcome is a random distribution without patterns, indicating that the linear regression model is appropriate. If patterns are present in the residual plot, it suggests that the model is not a suitable choice. The discussion will continue on constant models and loss functions in the next session. Additionally, methods for evaluating model fit include visualizing data, computing statistics like means and standard deviations, and using performance metrics such as Root Mean Square Error (RMSE).

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The last thing that we will do, and this is what you will see in discussions, is we will visualize your residuals. So when we go ahead and visualize residuals, what we want to see is that if these are the four different data sets that I'm dealing with and I'm passing the regression line through them, you can kind of see that the regression line matches one or two of them perfectly, but it's not a suitable choice for the rest of them. If you go ahead and visualize the residual, meaning that on the y-axis, you plot the residuals and the x-axis, you plot x, we don't want this residual plot to have any kind of pattern. Which is what you're seeing in this first case that residual plot does not show any pattern, but you're seeing that residual plot absolutely shows the pattern, meaning that linear regression was not a good choice. Or residual plot, in this case also shows the pattern. also in this case shows the pattern, right? So what we want at the end is that once you build the residual plot, you really want to make sure that the errors are kind of randomly distributed and equally distributed across X and also across the residual kind of dimension. Your T's are going to more talk about this a little bit more in the discussion. Thank you for coming. We're going to talk about constant model and more loss functions on Thursday.
